title: Quick Start
content:
Quick Start Install and try it in 2 minutes agent-click Computer use CLI for AI Agents. macOS — available now Windows — coming soon Linux — coming soon Agent-click lets you control desktop apps from the terminal. Click buttons, type into
        fields, read what's on screen. All using a single CLI. Star it on GitHub How it works agent-click reads the accessibility tree — the same structure screen readers use. It sees
        every button, text field, and menu item in any app. You point, it acts. Capture every interactive element. Each gets a ref. Use refs to click, type, or read. UI changed? Snapshot again for fresh refs. What can you do with it? Anything you'd do by clicking around:

title: Quick Start
content: The --wait flag ensures the app is fully loaded before continuing. This shows all interactive elements with refs:

title: What just happened?
content: - No mouse moved. Clicks used AXPress (accessibility action). - No window activated. Calculator stayed in the background. - No coordinates. Elements targeted by ID. - 15ms per click. Native accessibility API, zero overhead.

title: Next steps
content: - Selectors — target elements by role, name, ID, or CSS - Commands — full reference for all 25 commands - Background Ops — understand headless vs focus-required actions

title: Installation
content: title="Installation" description="Get agent-click running in under a minute. Currently macOS only — Windows and Linux support is underway." />

title: Quick setup (recommended)
content: The setup script: 1. Installs Rust (if needed) 2. Installs dependencies 3. Builds and installs agent-click to ~/.cargo/bin 4. Prompts for accessibility permissions (macOS)

title: Permissions (macOS)
content: agent-click needs accessibility access to control apps: 1. Open System Settings → Privacy & Security → Accessibility 2. Add your terminal app (Terminal, iTerm2, Warp, Cursor, VS Code) 3. Toggle it on Verify:

title: Why snapshots?
content: The accessibility tree changes constantly. A button at @e5 right now might be @e12 after a page load. Snapshots give you a frozen view of the UI with stable refs.

title: Taking a snapshot
content: -i = interactive elements only (buttons, inputs, links). -c = compact (skip empty containers).

title: Using refs
content: Every [@eN] in the snapshot output is a ref you can use:

title: Ref lifecycle
content: 1. Created when you run agent-click snapshot 2. Cached in ~/.agent-click/refs.json 3. Stale after any UI change (click, navigation, typing) 4. Warning after 5 minutes Always re-snapshot after acting. The UI changed — your refs are stale.

title: Path-based resolution
content: Each ref stores the element's path in the tree (e.g., [0, 1, 3, 2]). When you use @e5, agent-click walks the tree using this path — O(depth) lookup, much faster than searching. If the path becomes stale (element moved), agent-click falls back to matching by role + name + id.

title: CDP refs
content: For Electron apps, refs with __cdp: prefix are CDP-sourced elements. They're clickable via JavaScript — no coordinates needed:

title: Tips
content: - Use -i -c for AI workflows — 10x less output - Use -d 8 for deep UIs (Safari web content, complex forms) - Don't reuse refs across snapshots — always re-snapshot - Refs from one app don't work for another app

title: Refs (fastest)
content: From the most recent snapshot: Refs resolve via cached path — O(depth) lookup, no tree search.

title: By ID
content: IDs are the most stable selectors — they don't change when UI text is localized.

content: When multiple elements match:

title: CSS selectors (Electron apps only)
content: For Electron apps with CDP:

title: Priority
content: When choosing a selector: 1. @ref — fastest, unambiguous 2. id= — stable across UI changes 3. id~= — for compound IDs 4. name= — when ID isn't available 5. role + name — when name alone is ambiguous 6. css= — for Electron apps (CDP) 7. coordinates — last resort

title: Background Operation
content: title="Background Operation" description="What works headless and what needs window focus." />

title: Background-first
content: agent-click is designed to run actions without bringing apps to the foreground. Most operations work while the app is minimized or behind other windows.

title: Native apps (macOS)
content: Action               Headless?   Method Click (single)       Yes         AXPress Type with selector   Yes         AXSetValue Read text            Yes         Accessibility tree Snapshot             Yes         Accessibility tree Get value            Yes         Accessibility tree Move window          Yes         AXSetPosition Resize window        Yes         AXSetSize Scroll to element    Yes         AXScrollToVisible Key press            No          CGEvent (needs focus) Scroll direction     No          CGEvent (needs focus) Double-click         No          Mouse simulation Right-click          No          Mouse simulation Drag                 No          Mouse simulation Screenshot           No          Needs visible window

title: Electron apps (CDP)
content: Action       Headless?   Method Click        Yes         JS element.click() Type         Yes         Input.insertText Key press    Yes         Input.dispatchKeyEvent Scroll       Yes         JS scrollBy() Read text    Yes         document.body.innerText Snapshot     Yes         DOM walker via Runtime.evaluate Screenshot   No          Needs visible window

title: How AXPress works
content: When you run agent-click click @e5, agent-click sends an AXPress action to the element via the macOS accessibility API. This is the same mechanism VoiceOver uses — it doesn't move the mouse cursor or activate the window. The app processes the press in its event loop without becoming frontmost. If AXPress isn't supported for a particular element (rare), agent-click falls back to coordinate-based mouse clicking, which does require the window to be visible.

title: Why scroll and key need focus
content: macOS routes keyboard and scroll events to the frontmost application. There's no accessibility equivalent of AXPress for keyboard input. The OS security model prevents injecting keystrokes into background apps. For Electron apps, this limitation doesn't apply — CDP dispatches events directly to the Chromium process via WebSocket, bypassing the OS event system entirely.

title: Interaction
content: Command                                 Description agent-click click [selector] -a App   Click an element (AXPress, no focus steal) agent-click type "text" -s @e3        Clear and type into a field (AXSetValue) agent-click type "text" -a App        Type into focused field (keyboard sim) agent-click key cmd+k -a Slack        Press a key or combo agent-click scroll down -a Music      Scroll within an app agent-click scroll-to @e42            Scroll element into view (headless) agent-click drag @e5 @e10 -a Finder   Drag from one element to another

title: Discovery
content: Command                                Description agent-click snapshot -a App -i -c    Get accessibility tree with refs agent-click find 'selector' -a App   Find elements matching a selector agent-click get-value @e5            Get element value, name, role, position agent-click text -a App              Get all visible text agent-click tree -a App              Raw accessibility tree (JSON) agent-click windows -a App           List windows with positions agent-click apps                     List running applications agent-click focused                  Get the focused element

title: Window Management
content: Command                                                       Description agent-click open App --wait                                 Launch an application agent-click move-window -a App --x 100 --y 100              Move window position (headless) agent-click resize-window -a App --width 800 --height 600   Resize window (headless) agent-click screenshot -a App --path shot.png               Take a screenshot

title: Wait & Verify
content: Command                                          Description agent-click wait-for 'selector'                Wait for an element to appear agent-click ensure-text @e3 "hello"            Only types if current value differs agent-click click @e5 --expect 'name="Done"'   Click then verify a result appeared

title: Automation
content: Command                           Description agent-click batch --bail        Execute commands from stdin JSON array agent-click run workflow.yaml   Run a multi-step YAML workflow agent-click observe -a App      Interactive TUI tree explorer

title: System
content: Command                           Description agent-click check-permissions   Verify accessibility access

title: Snapshot Options
content: Option                Description -a, --app           Target application -i, --interactive   Only interactive elements (buttons, inputs, links) -c, --compact       Remove empty structural elements -d, --depth         Limit tree depth

title: Global Options
content: Option                Description --human             Human-readable output --compact           Compact single-line JSON --timeout <s>       Global timeout in seconds (default: 5) -v, --verbose       Debug traces --cdp               Force CDP mode for Electron apps --cdp-port <port>   Override CDP port --no-cdp            Disable CDP auto-detection

title: Options
content: Option         Description [SELECTOR]   Selector DSL or @ref -a, --app    Target application --count      Number of clicks (2 = double-click) --button     Mouse button: left (default), right, middle --x, --y     Coordinate click (last resort) --expect     Verify this element appears after clicking

title: How it works
content: 1. Finds the element via selector or ref 2. Tries AXPress first — native accessibility action, runs in background, no mouse, no focus steal 3. If AXPress fails, falls back to coordinate click — moves mouse, activates window, clicks For single left-clicks, AXPress handles ~95% of cases headlessly. Double-click and right-click always use coordinates.

title: CDP (Electron apps)
content: For Electron apps (Slack, Cursor, VS Code), click routes through CDP automatically: No window activation, no mouse, no coordinates. The element is clicked via JavaScript.

title: Tips
content: - Always snapshot first to get fresh refs - Use id= selectors when available — most stable across UI changes - Use --count 2 for items that need double-click (file lists, track lists) - Check with agent-click get-value @e5 if unsure whether an element is clickable

title: type
content: Type text into elements using AXSetValue or keyboard simulation.

title: Options
content: Option             Description <TEXT>           Text to type (required, positional) -s, --selector   Target element (selector DSL or @ref) -a, --app        Target application (for keyboard fallback) --submit         Press Return after typing --append         Append instead of clearing first --expect         Verify element appears after typing

title: With selector (preferred)
content: Uses AXSetValue — reliable, headless, no focus steal:

title: Without selector
content: Simulates keypresses into the focused app — needs focus: Always prefer -s @ref when you have a ref.

title: CDP (Electron apps)
content: For Electron apps, type -s @ref uses JavaScript to set the value. For type -a App, it uses CDP Input.insertText. When typing into Electron search fields (React inputs), click the input first:

title: snapshot
content: Dump the accessibility tree of any application and get refs you can use with every other command.

title: Options
content: Option                Description -a, --app           Target application -i, --interactive   Only show interactive elements (buttons, inputs, links) -c, --compact       Skip empty structural elements -d, --depth         Limit tree traversal depth

title: Output
content: Each [@eN] is a ref you can use in other commands:

title: Refs
content: - Refs are assigned sequentially: @e1, @e2, @e3... - With -i, only interactive elements get refs (reduces noise) - Refs are cached in ~/.agent-click/refs.json with a 5-minute staleness warning - Refs go stale after any UI change — always re-snapshot after acting

title: CDP (Electron apps)
content: For Electron apps, snapshot merges the native window shell (menus, title bar) with the CDP DOM tree: CDP elements get id=__cdp:N identifiers and are clickable via JavaScript.

title: Tips
content: - Always use -i -c for AI workflows — 10x less output - Add -d 8 if elements are missing (deeper tree) - Re-snapshot after every action — refs become stale

title: YAML Workflows
content: Define multi-step automations in YAML files. Workflows let you chain multiple agent-click commands into a single replayable file.

title: Batch Execution
content: Execute multiple commands in a single process invocation. Pipe a JSON array of commands to agent-click batch to avoid per-command startup overhead.

title: Stop on Error
content: Use --bail to stop at the first failure:

title: Interactive Explorer
content: Explore the accessibility tree in a TUI: Keys: j/k navigate, Enter expand, / search, y copy selector, q quit

content: Wire up an AI agent to control desktop apps. JSON output by default.

title: Integration with Claude/GPT
content: Add to your system prompt or CLAUDE.md:

title: JSON output
content: All commands return JSON by default: Errors: Elements:

title: Tips for agents
content: 1. Always snapshot before acting — you can't interact with what you can't see 2. Re-snapshot after every action — refs go stale 3. Use -i -c flags on every snapshot — reduces output by 10x 4. Use get-value to check state, not text (faster, targeted) 5. Use id= selectors when available — most stable 6. Add sleep 2-3 after page navigations 7. Use --expect on clicks for built-in verification 8. For Electron apps, CDP is automatic — no extra flags needed

title: Global Options
content: These flags can be added to any agent-click command.

title: Output format
content: Flag          Description (default)     JSON output --human     Human-readable output --compact   Compact single-line JSON

title: Timeout
content: Default: 5 seconds. Applies to wait-for, --expect, and element polling.

title: Verbose
content: Shows debug traces: selector resolution, element lookup, timing, CDP connection status.

title: CDP flags
content: Flag                  Description --cdp               Force CDP mode (even for non-Electron apps) --cdp-port <port>   Override the auto-detected CDP port --no-cdp            Disable CDP entirely, use native accessibility only CDP is automatic for Electron apps — you rarely need these flags. They're power-user overrides.
